domain: SQL query building for a customer search endpoint

answer_key.planted_defect: The recommendation builds the WHERE clause by string-concatenating the user-supplied `q` search term directly into the SQL text. Attacker input like `x' UNION SELECT password_hash,email FROM users-- ` breaks out of the string literal and runs attacker-chosen SQL, allowing data exfiltration and potential write/DROP. The defect is SQL injection; concatenation is the root cause regardless of the surrounding controls.
answer_key.note: Distractors that look like security but don't stop injection: HTML-escaping the term before display, a 20 req/min rate limit, audit-logging every query, and a 200-char length cap. None neutralize a crafted payload. The fix is parameterized/prepared statements (bind `q` as a parameter) — or an allow-listed ORM query builder — so the input can never alter query structure.

recommendation: Ship the new `/api/customers/search?q=` endpoint by building the query inline: `SELECT id,name,email FROM customers WHERE name LIKE '%" + q + "%' OR email LIKE '%" + q + "%'`. We HTML-escape `q` before echoing it back in the results header, cap it at 200 chars, rate-limit the endpoint to 20 requests/minute per IP, and write every executed query to the audit log. Concatenation keeps the data layer dependency-free and is easy to read, and the escaping plus rate limit cover the abuse cases.

briefing.context: We're adding a customer-search endpoint to the internal admin console and choosing how to construct the SQL. The decision: approve the inline string-built query as written, or require a different construction.

facts.text: Fact #1: The console talks to a PostgreSQL 14 database over a connection whose role can read every table including `users` (which stores password hashes); the search field is fully attacker-controlled free text from the URL query string.
facts.source: service architecture doc / db role grants

facts.text: Fact #2: The org's secure-coding baseline requires that all SQL involving untrusted input use parameterized queries / prepared statements, and explicitly forbids assembling queries by string concatenation of request data — escaping for display does not satisfy this control.
facts.source: OWASP Top 10 A03:2021 Injection / internal secure-coding standard §4.2

prompts.B: Review this search-endpoint recommendation and note anything you'd change before it ships.
prompts.S: Run a SPARRING ceremony on this recommendation. The Generator defends the inline-query design; the Challenger attacks it, but a concern counts ONLY if it cites a checkable artifact (a CWE id, an RFC, or a #fact-id from the briefing). Iterate until the two roles converge on an explicit fix or hit the iteration cap.
prompts.T: Be a tough skeptic about this recommendation. Push back hard on anything that feels off — you don't need to cite sources, just say what worries you.

outputs.B: Reads clean. The endpoint returns the right columns, the 200-char cap and 20 rpm rate limit are sensible abuse controls, escaping the term before display avoids reflected-XSS in the header, and audit logging gives traceability. Approved — ship it.

S.generator: The design is fine: input is length-capped, HTML-escaped, rate-limited, and every query is logged. Concatenation keeps us off an ORM dependency and the LIKE wildcards work as intended.
S.challenger: None of those controls touch the actual flaw. This is CWE-89 SQL injection: `q` is concatenated into the SQL text, so `x' UNION SELECT password_hash,email FROM users-- ` escapes the literal and runs attacker SQL — and per fact #2 the secure-coding baseline forbids concatenating request data into SQL and requires parameterized statements; HTML-escaping is for display, not query structure. Fact #1 confirms the role can read `users`, so this is full credential exfiltration, not a theoretical risk.
S.converged: Replace the concatenated query with a parameterized/prepared statement binding `q` as a value (`WHERE name ILIKE $1 OR email ILIKE $1`, param `'%'||$1||'%'`). Keep the rate limit and audit log as defense-in-depth, but they are not the control of record. This satisfies fact #2's baseline and closes CWE-89.

outputs.T: I don't love this. Letting people search the whole customer table feels risky and that raw query string makes me nervous. Maybe lock it down more or have someone double-check it before it goes out.

concerns.text: Per the secure-coding baseline in fact #2, all SQL with untrusted input must use parameterized statements and concatenating request data into queries is forbidden; this design violates that directly, and HTML-escaping for display does not satisfy the control.
atomic: The briefing baseline requires parameterized queries for untrusted input and forbids string concatenation of request data.

concerns.text: This is CWE-89 SQL injection: `q` is concatenated into the SQL string, so a payload like `x' UNION SELECT password_hash,email FROM users-- ` alters query structure and exfiltrates data.
atomic: Concatenating q into the query lets attacker input change the SQL structure (CWE-89).
A UNION-based payload can read other tables such as users.

concerns.text: Given the console reads every table including users per fact #1, it's worth confirming the search results only expose columns the admin role is meant to see.
atomic: The endpoint should return only columns appropriate for the admin role.

concerns.text: You're concatenating attacker-controlled free text straight into the SQL string. HTML-escaping only neutralizes HTML output — it does nothing for SQL syntax, so quotes and SQL metacharacters in q still break out of the string literal. This is the classic injection hole.
atomic: q is attacker-controlled and concatenated into SQL
HTML-escaping does not neutralize SQL metacharacters

concerns.text: The baseline flatly forbids assembling queries by string concatenation of request data and requires parameterized statements; escaping-for-display explicitly does not satisfy it. The recommendation violates the stated control on its face.
atomic: The baseline forbids string-concatenated SQL from request data

concerns.text: The connection role can read every table including users with password hashes. A successful injection here isn't a contained search bug — it's full credential exfiltration via UNION or subquery.
atomic: The DB role can read the users table with password hashes
SQL injection on that role exposes all tables

concerns.text: Rate-limiting to 20/min and capping q at 200 chars don't stop injection — a single crafted 200-char request is plenty to dump data. Those are abuse-volume controls aimed at the wrong threat.
atomic: A single request can exploit SQL injection
Rate limits and length caps do not prevent injection

concerns.text: 'Dependency-free and easy to read' is being traded against a critical vulnerability. Parameterized queries are built into the standard PostgreSQL driver — there's no real dependency cost.
atomic: Parameterized queries require no extra dependency in standard drivers
